# Abstract {-}

The integration of complex, multi-modal datasets requires a delicate balance between extracting non-linear structure and maintaining mechanistic interpretability. In this work, we present **pysimlr**, a unified framework that bridges classical Similarity-driven Multi-view Linear Reconstruction (SiMLR) with modern deep learning via the **Non-negative Stiefel approximating flow**. We demonstrate that by utilizing the Non-negative Stiefel approximating flow with a soft-orthogonality weight ($w=0.1$), we can discover statistically independent latent features that remain highly predictive even when used as standalone linear projectors. We introduce the **LEND** (Linear Encoder, Nonlinear Decoder) architecture, which leverages deep decoders to "explain away" non-linear noise, thereby improving the fidelity of linear feature discovery. Through extensive synthetic benchmarks and real-world multi-modal applications, we prove that this framework allows researchers to navigate the interpretability-performance frontier, delivering linear projectors that are both biologically meaningful and task-generalizable.
